does: This paper introduces RouteScan, a non-intrusive safety auditing framework for Mixture-of-Experts language models that operates entirely through GPU-level expert routing telemetry without requiring access to user inputs or outputs. The core contribution is demonstrating that the sparse activation patterns of MoE experts during model execution leave detectable footprints in low-level GPU metrics such as the number of active GPU threads allocated to expert modules during the prefilling phase. The method uses these micro-architectural signals as discriminative features to classify whether a model is processing harmful versus benign content. By working at the GPU telemetry layer, RouteScan enables safety monitoring while preserving user privacy, addressing a fundamental tension in content-based auditing approaches that typically require examining sensitive model inputs and outputs to detect harmful behaviors.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of MoE model interpretability and safety auditing research. It extends prior work on understanding sparse expert routing mechanisms in scaled language models and connects to research on side-channel analysis in machine learning systems. The paper responds to limitations in existing content-based safety auditing methods that require intrusive access to prompts, inputs, or outputs, addressing concerns raised in privacy-preserving ML and federated learning communities. It leverages insights from micro-architectural analysis and side-channel inference techniques developed in security research, applying them to the novel domain of LLM safety monitoring. The work also builds on emerging literature examining whether model computation patterns leak information about input content, extending this to the safety auditing context with MoE-specific routing telemetry.
enables: This work opens new directions for privacy-preserving safety auditing of large-scale deployed models without requiring content access. It enables continuous runtime monitoring of MoE models in production environments while maintaining user privacy guarantees, potentially allowing multiple stakeholders to audit deployed systems simultaneously. The approach suggests broader applications of GPU telemetry analysis for detecting model misbehaviors beyond safety, including detecting data exfiltration or unauthorized model behavior. Future work could extend this framework to other sparse model architectures, develop adversarial defenses against telemetry-based auditing, or combine routing telemetry with other non-intrusive signals for multi-modal safety verification. The method may also inspire new interpretability techniques that operate purely on model execution patterns rather than content, potentially enabling privacy-preserving analysis of other aspects of model behavior in production settings.
